name: master stuffed poblanos
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS. Both use poblanos halved (not whole-stuffed) and roasted without parboiling. Cream cheese is the binding fat in both — non-negotiable.

SHARED PREP:
- Preheat oven to 400.
- Halve the poblanos lengthwise and seed them. Don't parboil — see notes.
- Start the poblanos baking while you make the filling.

VARIANT A — CREAM CHEESE AND SEEDS (american / mexican-leaning):
- Toast a mix of pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds, and/or sesame seeds in olive oil with minced garlic until fragrant.
- Add chia seeds off-heat (they don't need toasting).
- Stir in garlic powder, onion powder, salt, and black pepper. Paprika or oregano if you want more mexican lean.
- Off heat, fold in cream cheese until the mixture comes together as a stuffable filling.
- Stuff the halves, return to the oven, finish until the cheese is bubbling and the poblano skin is blistered.

VARIANT B — WHITE BEAN AND INDIAN/MASSAMAN (priya krishna):
- Sauté garlic in olive oil with massaman curry paste (or your indian spice base — turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala) until fragrant.
- Add white beans and cook, mashing them as you go. Add splashes of milk as needed to keep the bottom from burning while you mash and cook the funk out of the beans.
- Don't add salt — the curry paste is usually salty enough on its own.
- When the bean paste tastes done (smooth, no raw-bean character), fold in cream cheese to taste — about 1/8 to 1/4 of a block, or two large spoonfuls. Don't be too delicate; the cream cheese balances the poblano.
- Stuff the halves, return to the oven, finish until done. Some bean paste will leak out — that's fine. Optionally peel the burned skin off when done.
notes:
POBLANO IS THE CENTER OF GRAVITY — DAIRY IS THE BINDER: in both variants, the poblano is the dish's structural and flavor anchor. The filling exists to bridge the pepper's smoky-grassy character with a creamy fat. That binder is cream cheese in both variants — no exceptions, no substitutions. Coconut milk doesn't work here, even in the massaman variant where it would normally pair beautifully; the poblano wants dairy, not coconut.

HALVE THE POBLANOS — DON'T STUFF WHOLE: whole-stuffing is naive and produces an annoying final presentation — the filling falls out, the pepper rolls around the plate, and you can't eat it cleanly with a fork. Halving lengthwise gives you flat, plate-friendly boats that hold the filling and present cleanly. This is the rule for poblanos specifically; bell peppers and onions can take whole-stuffing, but poblanos don't.

NEVER PARBOIL THE POBLANOS — SOG FACTOR: stuffed bell peppers and stuffed onions sometimes get parboiled to soften before the bake. Don't do that with poblanos. Poblanos get soggy faster than the thicker-walled bell peppers and onions; parboiling collapses their structure and you end up with limp pepper skins around the filling instead of a roasted, blistered shell. Roast them dry from the start.

WHY POBLANO, NOT JALAPEÑO: poblanos are the right pepper for this dish format — wall thickness for stuffing, heat low enough to eat as a main, and a smoky-grassy character that bridges with dairy. Baked jalapeños (the obvious alternative for a stuffed-pepper dish) are unpleasant: they're too spicy as a main, and the act of baking them releases capsaicin and pepper aerosols that irritate eyes and lungs. Not worth the kitchen suffering. Poblanos give you the same stuffed-pepper format without that downside.

VARIANT A NOTES:

- SEEDS ARE THE PROTEIN: pumpkin seeds (pepitas) are canonical; sunflower seeds and sesame seeds also work. Chia seeds add a subtle thickening to the cream cheese binder. Mix freely — no rigid axis here.
- OCCIDENTAL SPICE BASE: garlic powder and onion powder are the workhorses. New mexican chili powder, paprika, and dried oregano lean it more mexican; black pepper and salt are non-negotiable.
- TOAST THE SEEDS FIRST: raw seeds in the filling read flat. Toasting in oil with garlic before folding in the cream cheese builds the savory depth this variant needs since it doesn't have curry paste or indian spices doing that work.
- OPTIONAL HIGHER-EFFORT PATH — PRE-ROAST AND PEEL: roast the poblano halves first, peel off the charred skin, THEN stuff with the filling and finish-roast. Smoother texture, deeper poblano flavor. More work, but the result is noticeably cleaner than the dry-roast-then-stuff path.
- PAIRS WITH WESTERN SALMON DISHES: the cream cheese filling gives this variant a natural pairing with western salmon preparations (french leek-and-cream salmon, lemon-butter salmon, gravlax-adjacent). The cream cheese in both the salmon plate and inside the pepper anchors the meal around the same fat profile without competing.

VARIANT B NOTES:

- MASSAMAN CURRY PASTE IS THE EASY PATH: massaman paste is essentially indian in spice profile (cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves) despite being a 'thai yellow curry paste.' It's the most indian thing on the thai curry shelf, and one jar does most of the spice work for you. Original recipe is from priya krishna.
- INDIAN SPICES INSTEAD OF PASTE — OPTIONAL: turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala can replace or supplement the massaman paste. Massaman is so flavorful on its own that the supplemental indian spices are probably irrelevant in most batches. Use them if your massaman paste is light, or for variety between batches.
- WHITE BEANS, NOT CHICKPEAS OR LENTILS: white beans mash into a smooth filling-shape; chickpeas stay too discrete and lentils go too loose. Stick with white beans here.
- MILK FOR DEGLAZING THE MASH: small splashes of milk during the mash keep the bottom from burning and help cook the funk out of the beans. The dairy slot is shared with the cream cheese — they're both binding the pepper to the filling.
